---
title: "View-key disclosure topology"
subtitle: "Selective decryption: who can read what, and how Aleo enforces it."
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status:** Stub. Full chapter coming soon.
:::

## The question

Two parties need to read an encrypted invoice. Add a tax auditor — now three
parties. Add the auditor's deputy — four. How do you grant decryption access
to a specific subset of users without re-encrypting the data each time?

## What this chapter will cover

- Aleo's view-key model: every address has an associated view key (32 bytes)
  that decrypts records owned by that address
- Wrapping a symmetric key K under N view keys → N parties can independently
  unwrap K and decrypt
- The "wrap once, append later" pattern: adding an auditor after the fact
  doesn't require re-encryption
- Why view keys cannot be revoked (and what to do if you need revocability:
  proxy re-encryption, threshold encryption — out of scope here)
- The off-chain "wrapped key vault": where the wrapped keys live and who
  can read them

## Interactive viz planned

A graph viz: nodes are parties (payer, merchant, auditor, observer), edges
represent "can decrypt this blob." Toggle parties on/off. Watch what becomes
visible to each party.

## Common anti-patterns

- Storing wrapped keys keyed by **address** in a public DB (re-leaks
  participant identity)
- Using non-AEAD encryption for the blob (allows undetectable corruption)
- Wrapping under the address itself (addresses don't have decryption capability;
  view keys do)
